localBiteReport: Reports from the past week or two out of Choctawhatchee Bay (Santa Rosa Beach/Destin/Freeport area) show speckled trout as the standout inshore species — guides around the bay are describing an active trout bite with numbers of fish and some quality fish mixed in. A large bull redfish (over 25 lbs) was also reported in the Santa Rosa Beach area within the last week, and charter catches in the bay have included redfish and flounder alongside trout. With water temps at 85°F, the summer pattern is fully in effect — early morning and late evening windows are most productive, with fish holding near grass flats, docks, and deeper bay structure through the heat of the day. Wind has been a factor in recent weeks, pushing anglers to be selective about which areas of the bay they fish, but when conditions cooperate the bite has been consistent.
localBiteSource: captainexperiences.com (Choctawhatchee Bay reports, reports from 6 days ago and ~2 weeks ago), fishingbooker.com (Choctawhatchee Bay charter catch reports, July 2026) · Auto-refreshed via Claude API

aiSummary: Speckled trout are the standout species across Choctawhatchee Bay right now, with guides reporting consistent numbers and quality fish mixed in — redfish and flounder have also been showing up in charter catches alongside the trout bite. With an 83% storm chance kicking in after 11 AM today, the morning window before low tide at 11:14 AM is your best and safest opportunity; plan to be off the water or tucked into protected water well before midday. Water temps are holding at 85°F, keeping the bite firmly in summer pattern — early is everything today.

bridge.aiNote: The 331 Bridge spans the narrow neck of Choctawhatchee Bay, creating a current funnel where bait gets pushed through on both tides. Pilings hold speckled trout, redfish, sheepshead, and black drum year-round, with the lee-side pilings serving as the prime ambush zone on incoming tides. This is open, exposed water — it's typically the first spot on the bay to become unfishable when wind picks up or weather builds.
bridge.todaysCall: With an east wind at 15 mph and an 83% storm chance after 11 AM, the 331 Bridge is a risky choice today — this exposed open-water spot turns rough quickly, and you won't have much margin before conditions deteriorate. If you go, get there at first light around 5:54 AM and work the lee-side pilings hard during the outgoing tide ahead of the 11:14 AM low. Slightly stained water and a first-quarter moon give the fish a moderate tidal push to work with — soft plastics and live shrimp tight to structure are the play. Plan to pull off no later than 10 AM given the incoming storm window; don't get caught here when lightning rolls in.

alaqua.aiNote: Alaqua Bayou offers protected water with a strong year-round redfish population, along with trout and flounder at the mouth. The oyster bars at the creek mouth are the prime target, and tree cover keeps the bayou a few degrees cooler than the open bay while providing shelter from wind and weather. The upper creek transitions toward brackish water and holds bass alongside smaller redfish.
alaqua.todaysCall: Alaqua is a smart pick today — the tree cover and protected layout will buffer the east 15 mph wind, and you can fish here comfortably well into the storm buildup window if you stay inside the bayou. Hit the oyster bars at the creek mouth early on the falling tide ahead of the 11:14 AM low, where redfish and trout should be stacking as water drains off the flats. Slightly stained water favors moving baits like gold spoons or paddle tails that fish can locate by vibration and contrast. If the bite slows before storms arrive, work back into the upper creek for small reds in the shade — the cooler water temps inside are a genuine advantage at 85°F bay-wide.

basin.aiNote: Basin Bayou sees less fishing pressure than nearby Alaqua and LaGrange, which tends to mean less-educated fish. The interior grass flats are classic sight-fishing territory for redfish in under two feet of water, and pompano occasionally show at the mouth on moving tides in late spring and early summer. It offers less weather protection than LaGrange when conditions build.
basin.todaysCall: Basin Bayou's lighter pressure makes it worth targeting today if you're after redfish on the flats, but keep the storm timeline firmly in mind — this spot offers less shelter than LaGrange when weather builds, so commit to an early-only plan. Work the interior grass flats on the falling tide before the 11:14 AM low; slightly stained water will push sight-fishing conditions toward the edge of workable, so lean on popping corks or weedless paddle tails over pure sight-casting. Be off the water or repositioned to more protected water by 10:30 AM at the latest given the 83% storm chance after 11 AM. The first-quarter moon is providing a moderate tidal push that should move bait and fish onto and off these shallow flats in a predictable pattern.

lagrange.aiNote: LaGrange Bayou is one of the top trout producers in this area, with legendary oyster bars at the mouth and interior grass flats that hold redfish year-round. Deep holes inside the bayou hold black drum and sheepshead, and the surrounding geography makes it the most protected water on this side of the bay. When weather builds, LaGrange is the go-to refuge for anglers who want to stay on fish without battling exposed conditions.
lagrange.todaysCall: LaGrange is the best overall call for today given the 83% storm chance after 11 AM — it's the most protected water on this side of the bay, meaning you can fish longer into the morning and have a safe harbor if storms arrive faster than forecast. Start at the oyster bars at the mouth right after sunrise at 5:54 AM, working the falling tide toward the 11:14 AM low for trout and redfish; the slightly stained water pairs well with chartreuse or root beer soft plastics under a popping cork. The bay-wide trout bite has been consistently strong, and LaGrange's oyster bar structure is exactly the kind of setup that's been producing. If the bite slows or storms push in early, drop into the deep interior holes for black drum and sheepshead on shrimp or fiddler crabs — you can keep fishing here when other spots are blown out.

fourmile.aiNote: Four Mile Creek is a short tidal creek running between Shipyard Marina and the bay. Tidal flow pushes baitfish up the creek on incoming tides, concentrating redfish and trout in the bends and grass edges, while the upper section transitions toward brackish water and holds bass alongside smaller reds. The creek mouth is a prime flounder zone, and the short, enclosed layout makes it the safest fishery on the bay when weather threatens.
fourmile.todaysCall: Four Mile Creek is arguably your safest option today — it's short, protected, and right off Shipyard Marina, so you can get on fish fast and get off the water fast if storms arrive ahead of schedule. Fish the creek mouth and lower bends on the falling tide before the 11:14 AM low, targeting flounder in the transition zone and reds and trout in the grass-edge bends. The east wind at 15 mph won't be a factor inside the creek, and the slightly stained water is actually a plus here — it concentrates fish in the cleaner tidal flow near structure rather than spreading them across flats. Given the 83% storm chance after 11 AM, the fact that you're already at the marina when it's time to pull the plug is a real logistical advantage today.

hogtown.aiNote: Hogtown Bayou is a signature Santa Rosa Beach guide spot and one of the most consistently productive trout flats on the bay. Summer mornings are peak season here, with trout stacked on grass in two to four feet of water, and Spanish mackerel show at the mouth on moving tides. It sees regular pressure but produces reliably, though anglers should factor in the longer run time compared to bayous closer to the 331 Bridge.
hogtown.todaysCall: Hogtown has been part of the active trout bite guides are reporting across the bay, and the summer morning pattern — trout stacked on grass flats in 2-4 feet — is exactly what today's early window sets up for. Leave the dock at first light to account for the longer run from Shipyard Marina and maximize time on the grass before the heat and storm risk build. Work the flats on the falling tide toward the 11:14 AM low with soft plastics or live shrimp under a cork; slightly stained water means fish are feeding by feel and proximity, so keep presentations in the strike zone and cover water. With an 83% storm chance after 11 AM, Hogtown's distance from the marina means you need to be heading back by 10 AM at the latest — this is not the day to push your luck on the return run. Spanish mackerel at the mouth are a bonus target on the outgoing tide if they're showing.

rocky.aiNote: Rocky Bayou sits inside Fred Gannon Rocky Bayou State Park near Niceville and sees significantly less pressure than bayous closer to the 331 Bridge. Freshwater influence from Rocky Creek makes flounder fishing excellent, and the glass-calm state park coves are well-suited to sight-fishing for redfish. Mangrove snapper hold near dock pilings, and the protected, scenic setting adds to its appeal — though it is the furthest location from Shipyard Marina and run time should be factored into any trip plan.
rocky.todaysCall: Rocky Bayou's protected state park setting and low pressure make it genuinely appealing on a day when weather is a factor, but the run time from Shipyard Marina is the critical variable today — with an 83% storm chance after 11 AM, be very deliberate about when you need to leave to make it back safely. If you commit to an early start at or before sunrise at 5:54 AM, you can work the calm coves for sight-fishing reds and target flounder in the freshwater-influenced zones on the falling tide ahead of the 11:14 AM low. Slightly stained water will make pure sight-casting tougher, so have weedless paddle tails or live shrimp ready as a backup for the flounder and reds. Mangrove snapper on the dock pilings are a reliable midmorning option if you're still on-site — they don't mind the cloud cover or stained water, and they'll hold tight to structure all day.
